claim: Early statistical language modeling can be framed as assigning probabilities to word sequences, with n-gram models estimating next-word likelihoods from short histories.
support_quality: context from Bengio paper discussion of statistical/n-gram language-model background
prose_permission: Allowed as high-level background; do not add exact benchmark or chronology claims without a separate source.
blockers: No claim that n-grams were the only pre-neural approach or that this chapter is a complete NLP history.

claim: Bengio et al. proposed a neural probabilistic language model using distributed word representations to address the curse of dimensionality in language modeling.
blockers: Do not claim it invented all embeddings or directly caused any specific later product without bridging sources.

claim: Mikolov et al. proposed efficient architectures for learning continuous word-vector representations from large datasets and evaluated them on similarity/analogy-style tasks.
blockers: Do not overstate analogy tasks as proof of human-like understanding.

claim: Sequence-to-sequence learning used encoder-decoder recurrent models to map input sequences to output sequences and showed strong machine-translation results.
blockers: Do not add exact BLEU/result claims until extracted from the paper.

claim: Bahdanau et al. introduced an alignment/attention mechanism for neural machine translation that let the decoder focus on relevant source positions rather than relying only on a fixed-length vector.
blockers: Do not treat attention weights as complete explanations of model behavior.

claim: The Transformer paper replaced recurrence/convolution in the core sequence transduction architecture with attention mechanisms and emphasized self-attention plus parallelizable training.
blockers: Do not claim the Transformer alone created later LLM products or that recurrence became obsolete everywhere.

claim: The chapter's historical synthesis is a bounded interpretive bridge from representations to contextual sequence models to attention-centered scalable architectures.
prose_permission: Allowed as chapter framing if kept qualitative.
blockers: No exact "first," "best," or state-of-the-art chronology claims without separate row-level support.

claim: The chapter treats vector analogies and attention weights as useful evidence/mechanisms but not as proof of human-like understanding or full behavioral explanation.
location: manuscript/01-before-the-transformer.md:Why Counting Was Not Enough;The Bottleneck Becomes A Plot Point
support_quality: negative-control interpretive guardrail tied to source-limited claims
blockers: Do not remove during prose polish; this prevents overclaiming from attractive demos.

claim: The chapter frames Transformer parallelism as infrastructure-relevant because removing recurrence from the core architecture made training more parallelizable across sequence positions.
location: manuscript/01-before-the-transformer.md:Why Parallelism Matters To A Book About Language
